industries.name: Cadmium Telluride Thin-Film Solar
uses: Cadmium telluride (CdTe) is the semiconductor absorber layer in First Solar's thin-film PV modules — CdTe has a near-ideal bandgap of 1.45 eV for solar energy conversion and can be deposited by close-space sublimation at high throughput
CdTe modules achieve 19–22% commercial module efficiency and have the lowest carbon footprint and water use of any PV technology in life-cycle analysis
Each GW of CdTe solar capacity requires approximately 60–90 tonnes of tellurium — at current installation rates this is a meaningful fraction of world tellurium supply (~500 t/yr)

Tellurium supply is critically constrained: it is recovered almost exclusively as a byproduct of electrolytic copper refining (from anode slimes) — global supply is structurally limited by copper refining capacity, not by mining
Tellurium supply scarcity is a key strategic risk for CdTe solar scale-up — driving First Solar to continuously reduce tellurium use per watt and invest in anode slime processing partnerships

companies.name: First Solar
companies.use: World's only large-scale CdTe thin-film solar manufacturer and by far the largest tellurium consumer. Operates gigawatt-scale CdTe module factories in Ohio (US), Malaysia, and Vietnam. Operates a closed-loop tellurium recycling program from end-of-life modules.

companies.name: 5N Plus
companies.use: Major producer of cadmium telluride semiconductor compound and refined tellurium metal for First Solar's thin-film solar production. Also produces other II-VI semiconductor compounds for infrared detection and specialty applications.

industries.name: Thermoelectric Devices
uses: Bismuth telluride (Bi₂Te₃) is the highest-performing room-temperature thermoelectric material — used in Peltier coolers (thermoelectric coolers, TECs) for CPU cooling, laser diode thermal management, portable refrigerators, and DNA thermocycler instruments

Bi₂Te₃-based thermoelectric generators convert waste heat to electricity in automotive exhaust systems, industrial processes, and remote power applications — BMW and Ford have demonstrated Bi₂Te₃ exhaust TEGs recovering 200–600W from exhaust heat

Lead telluride (PbTe) thermoelectric modules for mid-temperature (400–700°C) waste heat recovery in industrial furnaces, and in NASA's radioisotope thermoelectric generators (RTGs) powering Voyager 1 & 2, Cassini, and New Horizons spacecraft

GeTe and TAGS (telluride of antimony, germanium, and silver) high-performance thermoelectrics for spacecraft power systems where long life and high efficiency at intermediate temperatures are critical

companies.name: Laird Technologies (Laird Thermal Systems)
companies.use: Major thermoelectric cooler (TEC) manufacturer. Produces Bi₂Te₃-based Peltier modules for laser diode cooling, laboratory instruments, medical diagnostics, and consumer electronics thermal management.

companies.name: Ferrotec
companies.use: Major Bi₂Te₃ thermoelectric module manufacturer supplying Peltier coolers for CPU cooling, DNA thermocyclers, portable refrigerators, and precision temperature-controlled optical and laser systems.

industries.name: Metallurgy & Specialty Alloys
uses: Tellurium addition to copper (0.3–0.7% Te) produces free-machining copper — tellurium forms copper telluride inclusions that act as chip breakers during machining, increasing cutting speeds by 50% vs. pure copper while retaining 90%+ of conductivity. Used for electrical connector contacts, bus bars, and precision machined electrical components
Lead telluride additions to free-machining lead steels and leaded brasses improve machinability without increasing lead content — tellurium modifies sulfide inclusion shape for better chip breaking
Tellurium improves rubber vulcanization: accelerates cure rates and improves aging resistance in chloroprene (neoprene) and natural rubber — used in high-performance seals, hoses, and cable jackets

Cadmium mercury telluride (CMT or HgCdTe): the dominant material for cooled infrared detectors in the 3–5 μm and 8–12 μm bands — used in military thermal imaging, missile seekers, space telescope focal planes (Hubble, James Webb NICMOS), and scientific infrared cameras

companies.name: Aurubis
companies.use: Europe's largest copper refiner and a major tellurium producer — tellurium is recovered from copper anode slimes generated during electrolytic copper refining. Supplies tellurium metal to CdTe solar, thermoelectric, and specialty alloy markets.

companies.name: Umicore
companies.use: Major tellurium refiner recovering tellurium from copper anode slimes and electronic scrap. Produces refined tellurium for CdTe solar, thermoelectric materials, and cadmium mercury telluride IR detector applications.

industries.name: Optical Data Storage
uses: Phase-change optical media (CD-RW, DVD-RW, Blu-ray RW) use tellurium-based alloys (GeTeSb, typically Ge₂Sb₂Te₅ or GST) as the recording layer — laser heating switches the alloy between amorphous (low reflectivity) and crystalline (high reflectivity) phases to record and erase data

Phase-change memory (PCM/PCRAM) using GST as the switching material for non-volatile semiconductor memory — Intel Optane XPoint used a phase-change material (likely GST or similar) for its 3D XPoint memory products
GST and related alloys in embedded phase-change memory for automotive microcontrollers, where high temperature retention and endurance outperform flash memory

companies.name: STMicroelectronics
companies.use: Leading producer of embedded phase-change memory (ePCM) for automotive microcontrollers and IoT applications. Uses Ge-Sb-Te alloy switching layers in its 90nm and 40nm ePCM processes for high-temperature automotive-grade non-volatile memory.
